_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Debug: Part 1
summary: Separate compile errors, runtime failures, wrong output, and hangs.
refresher: Compiler errors prevent a build, runtime failures happen during execution, and logic errors finish with the wrong answer. Start with the earliest useful diagnostic and reproduce one case.

blocks.body: Compiler errors prevent a build, runtime failures happen during execution, and logic errors finish with the wrong answer. Start with the earliest useful diagnostic and reproduce one case. Separate compile errors, runtime failures, wrong output, and hangs.

C++ failures fall into build errors, runtime symptoms, wrong answers, and hangs. Runtime symptoms can include undefined behavior, where the language no longer promises any particular result. A program that seems to work after invalid memory access is still broken, so warnings and boundary checks matter.

blocks.caption: Subtracting one selects the final valid position before checked access.
Prefer checked access while locating an index failure.

blocks.body: The wrong index equals the element count and sits one position beyond the end. The corrected expression is valid only when the vector is nonempty, so add that guard for general input. A thrown range error is more informative than silent memory misuse.

blocks.title: Inspect this state
blocks.body: Compiler messages can cascade from one missing symbol. Fix the first reported source location, rebuild, and then reconsider the remaining messages.

blocks.prompt: What kind of issue is a missing semicolon?
blocks.why: The compiler cannot parse the statement boundary, so no executable is produced.

blocks.prompt: What does `.at(2)` do for two elements?
blocks.why: The valid positions are zero and one, and checked access reports position two as out of range.
